title: ExoVeil: How to Predict a Star and Find an Invisible Planet
oneliner: The ExoVeil system learns to predict a star's brightness and detects single transits that were previously invisible.
description: Normally, detecting [tag:exoplanet]exoplanets[/tag] requires multiple transits, but ExoVeil uses self-supervised learning to spot a single shadow. This opens the door to planets with long orbits, including Earth analogs.

title: Neutrino Fingerprints: Invisible Traces Turn into Pictures
oneliner: Converting IceCube detector data into RGB images allows a neural network to accurately determine neutrino direction, using just 15 kilobytes per event.
description: Researchers have developed a method to encode IceCube neutrino observatory events into compact images (72×72 pixels). A ResNet18 convolutional neural network processes these 'neutrino fingerprints' and reconstructs the particle's direction with an average error of 1.10 radians, only slightly behind

title: Stellar Alchemy: How Carbon Chains Become Building Blocks for Planets
oneliner: Astronomers have found a long carbon chain HC5N in a protoplanetary disk — it's like finding a complex ingredient in an uncooked cosmic soup.
description: Using the ALMA radio telescope, the molecule HC5N — one of the longest carbon chains known in such objects — has been detected for the first time in the disk around the young star TW Hya. This discovery shows that complex organic chemistry is possible even in cold planet-forming regions, providing a

title: Nereid: The Last Fragment of Neptune's Lost Family
oneliner: New JWST data and simulations reveal that Nereid is not a captured interloper but a surviving regular moon of Neptune, enduring after Triton's catastrophic invasion.
description: Nereid, Neptune's third-largest moon, was long thought to be a captured Kuiper Belt object. But JWST spectroscopy and dynamical modeling unveiled its true origin: it was born alongside the planet, but Triton's gravity flung it into a mad orbit upon capture. Now it's the sole surviving 'native' moon,

title: Conductor of the Cosmic Orchestra: How to Synchronize Telescopes to Femtosecond Precision
oneliner: A new combiner panel for radio interferometers acts like a virtuoso conductor, synchronizing hundreds of telescopes with sub-picosecond precision.
description: A high-performance panel for the CRS platform has been developed, capable of processing 128 inputs at 25 Gbps per line. It provides delay stability at the femtosecond level, opening the path to studying dark matter and the early Universe.

title: Mass of the Neutron Star in 4U 1820–30: A Fresh Perspective
oneliner: Analysis of gravitational redshift, pulsations, and bursts reveals contradictions in neutron star mass estimates.
description: The article revisits the mass of the neutron star in the ultracompact binary system 4U 1820–30, incorporating a recently discovered absorption line at 3.8 keV interpreted as a gravitationally redshifted iron line. It is shown that the derived redshift 1+z=1.72 implies high compactness, which is diff

title: Cosmic Twist: How the Universe Twists Light
oneliner: A new method has measured a hint of a strange effect — cosmic birefringence — which could open a door to dark matter.
description: Scientists have found a way to separate the true cosmic signal from instrumental noise. Using a hybrid ILC, they noticed that the polarization of the cosmic microwave background is slightly rotated — by only a third of a degree. This is like a hint of new physics.

title: Whisper of the Earth: AI Seismologist Protects Gravitational Waves
oneliner: A transformer-based neural network, SeismoGPT, learned to predict seismic waves, which could aid future gravitational wave detectors.
description: SeismoGPT is a 26-million parameter causal transformer that predicts seismograms. The model achieves a median NCC above 0.93, paving the way for active suppression of seismic noise in gravitational wave observatories.

title: The Culinary Secret of Neutron Stars
oneliner: A new model shows that the cooling of neutron stars after outbursts is explained by thermonuclear cooking in the shell, not by mysterious 'surface heating'.
description: Astrophysicists have solved a long-standing puzzle: why neutron stars cool slower after accretion than models predicted. It turns out the key lies in thermonuclear burning of hydrogen and helium in the shell, acting like an 'inner kitchen' that sustains heat. This discovery removes the need for an a
